About.title: About Me
About.description1: I am a backend engineer specializing in .NET and C#, focused on building APIs, modular backends, and clean architecture. I deliver reliable systems, resolve production incidents, and improve performance through incremental, well-structured changes.
About.description2: I care about maintainability, modularity, and clear design over just 'shipping' code. I have experience leading technical teams, performing code reviews, and establishing architectural standards. I automate workflows with CI/CD (GitHub Actions + Docker) and use AI-assisted tools for support, while always maintaining full responsibility for system design and validation.

hero.title: Kevin Martínez
hero.quote: Scaling isn't the problem. Uncontrolled complexity is.
hero.plainStatement: Software Engineer primarily focused on backend, systems design, tooling, and architecture on top of .NET.
hero.description: I build software that has to survive real constraints: production traffic, integrations, legacy decisions, data consistency, and operational maintenance. My primary focus is backend, systems, and architecture, but I also cover React frontend work, tooling, CI/CD, and Python services when the problem calls for it.

about.title: Practical Architecture
paragraphs: Backend, Architecture, and decision making under constraints. I don't build for hypothetical scale; I build for predictable evolution.

I work on systems that are already running in production or will be. That means dealing with constraints, legacy decisions, real business impact, and covering multiple layers when needed: backend, data, delivery, tooling, and React UI.
Architecture is not about drawing boxes, it is about controlling how complexity evolves over time.

items.title: Domain over Framework
items.description: Frameworks are implementation details. Business logic should not depend on them. If changing a framework breaks your system, the system was never properly designed.

items.title: Boundaries over Coupling
items.description: Shared databases are the original sin of system design. Without clear ownership and contracts, modularity is just an illusion.

items.title: Simplicity before Complexity
items.description: Microservices are not a solution, they are a cost. If a monolith is not modular, distributing it will only amplify the problem.

items.title: Clarity over Over-Abstraction
items.description: Not every abstraction improves a system. When it only hides explicit code, it usually adds layers, indirection, and cognitive cost without solving a real problem.

caseStudies.title: Case Studies
caseStudies.subtitle: [EXPANDABLE_ARCHIVE]
caseStudies.footerHint: /* expand() to inspect constraints, decisions, and outcomes */

items.title: From Clean Architecture to a modular monolith in a multi-tenant CRM
items.summary: Architectural evolution of a business backend that stopped being simple.
items.problem: The CRM backend had to support customer management, companies, operations, and integration with a retail ecosystem. The initial base worked, but domain growth and new integrations started to strain navigation, coupling, and maintainability.
items.decision: Evolve from a classic Clean Architecture baseline into a modular backend with explicit module contracts, CQRS, and domain events, prioritizing controlled growth over premature distribution.

items.title: Company onboarding orchestration with distributed consistency
items.summary: Reliable company creation flow coordinating multiple systems with compensating actions.
items.problem: Creating a company was not a simple database operation. The flow had to generate credentials, create the user in AWS Cognito, persist the local user, create the company, and trigger credential delivery. If any step failed, the platform could end up in an inconsistent state, such as a Cognito user with no associated company.
items.decision: An orchestrated saga was implemented to execute each step in order, persist workflow state, and apply compensating actions on failure. The operation was protected against duplicate retries, and credential delivery was decoupled from the main flow through asynchronous processing.

items.title: Mandatory updates for a production WinForms plugin
items.summary: Controlled version delivery with integrity validation and release automation.
items.problem: Clients running outdated versions created operational inconsistencies and increased support cost. The system needed an update strategy that was reliable, automated, and hard to bypass.
items.decision: Build an automatic mandatory update system with background downloads, hash validation, and GitHub Actions / releases support, preserving operational continuity and version control.

items.title: Forced microservices rewrite without real context
items.summary: A case where architecture was defined before the problem was understood.
items.problem: A legacy PHP system had to be replaced entirely with a new .NET solution. The initial decision was to move directly into a microservices architecture without validating whether the system context actually required it.
items.decision: A full rewrite based on microservices was chosen from the start, introducing multiple services, distributed communication, and higher operational complexity without a stable functional base.

items.name: Html2Pdf
items.description: .NET library for turning HTML into PDF when a backend needs repeatable document generation without duplicating rendering logic per project.
details: Solves document generation from HTML templates without rebuilding rendering pipelines in every system.
Uses RazorLight and PuppeteerSharp to keep output consistent for reports, receipts, and operational documents.
Fits service flows, jobs, or batch processes where PDF is a cross-cutting capability instead of an isolated feature.
items.intent: Turn PDF generation into reusable backend infrastructure for systems that produce documents under business rules.

items.name: SimpleMediator
items.description: Lightweight Mediator/CQRS implementation for .NET for systems that need command/query separation without paying for ornamental complexity.
details: Solves common boilerplate in .NET applications built around handlers, requests, and simple composition.
Exists to keep write/read boundaries explicit without depending on heavier packages when the problem does not justify them.
Used in modular backends, business APIs, and internal libraries where structural clarity matters more than endless extension points.
items.intent: Reduce accidental overhead in small and medium CQRS implementations while keeping the structure explicit.

items.name: Flask Forge CLI
items.description: CLI for bootstrapping Flask projects when Python is the right runtime for a service or developer tool and the starting point must stay consistent.
details: Solves repetitive project setup by generating structure, conventions, and baseline configuration ready to evolve.
Exists to avoid inconsistent manual setup and to shorten the path from idea to working service.
Used for small APIs, internal utilities, or tools where Python/Flask fits the problem better than forcing the primary stack everywhere.
items.intent: Standardize the starting point of Flask projects and make setup a repeatable operation.

items.name: light-query
items.description: React hooks library for remote data flows with a small API and a deliberate focus on reducing integration noise.
details: Wraps fetch, loading states, and basic synchronization so components do not repeat transport plumbing.
Keeps the surface area intentionally small for teams that want control without adopting a heavier caching runtime.
Fits React frontends where clarity of integration matters more than maximizing abstraction coverage.
items.intent: Simplify API consumption in React without hiding the actual data flow or adding accidental complexity.

items.name: dotnet-clean-template
items.description: Native .NET template pack that bootstraps Clean Architecture projects through `dotnet new`, avoiding repeated base structure, cross-cutting decisions, and setup work in every backend.
details: Generates an opinionated foundation with Application, Domain, Infrastructure, SharedKernel, Web.Api, and ArchitectureTests ready to evolve.
Includes JWT authentication, PBKDF2 hashing, EF Core with PostgreSQL, Problem Details, health checks, and validation/logging decorators without depending on MediatR.
Provides two starting points: one oriented to Docker Compose for local infrastructure and another based on .NET Aspire for orchestration and service defaults.
items.intent: Turn .NET API bootstrap into a repeatable operation with consistent architectural standards and less accidental boilerplate.

items.name: Vanilla SPA
items.description: Dependency-free SPA architecture used to test routing, state, and UI composition on top of browser-native APIs.
details: Measures how much runtime a framework is actually buying compared with an organized native baseline.
Implements routing, a reactive store, and component composition with ES Modules and minimal tooling.
Has a public deployment, but the main role is frontend architecture exploration rather than general production adoption.
items.intent: Explore how far a real SPA can be pushed with native primitives before introducing a framework.

items.name: mini-php-framework
items.description: PHP micro-framework for exploring a minimal core with routing, middleware, and SQLite persistence under PSR conventions.
details: Isolates the basics of a request pipeline without starting from a full framework stack.
Uses PSR standards to preserve interoperability while testing a deliberately small and explicit base.
Has Packagist distribution, but its role is to investigate lightweight framework decisions rather than package a closed platform.
items.intent: Test the minimum viable set of backend framework decisions to understand the real cost of abstraction in PHP.

items.title: Modular monolith before premature microservices
items.body: As the system grew, the real need was to control boundaries and internal navigation, not to distribute problems across the network. Explicit modularity was prioritized before physical extraction.

items.title: CQRS to isolate domain evolution
items.body: The same model no longer served equally well for business rule writes and operational reads. Separating reads and writes made domain changes safer.

items.title: Shared data access across modules
items.body: Sharing tables or bypassing module boundaries looked practical, but it hid dependencies and made local changes unsafe.

items.title: Orchestrated saga for onboarding with multiple side effects
items.body: When creating a company touches identity, database state, and notifications, a local transaction is no longer enough. Explicit orchestration, compensations, and idempotency were prioritized over accidental consistency.

items.title: OpenTelemetry as a common observability language
items.body: When each component explains failures differently, diagnostics under pressure become expensive. Unifying traces and metrics improves system readability.

stack.title: Stack Protocols
stack.footerHint: /* the stack follows the problem, not the vendor */

items.name: C# / .NET
items.detail: Primary environment for backend, APIs, and system design with focus on structural clarity and controlled evolution.

items.name: SQL Server / PostgreSQL / MySQL
items.detail: Relational modeling, query optimization, and support for production systems across different operational contexts.

items.name: EF Core / Dapper
items.detail: A balance between productivity and control depending on the access pattern and the level of precision the system needs.

items.name: AWS
items.detail: Use of SNS, Cognito, S3, and SES for messaging, authentication, and infrastructure support in distributed flows.

items.name: Azure
items.detail: Use of Azure DevOps, Application Insights, SQL Server, PostgreSQL, Cosmos DB, Virtual Machines, App Service, and API Management in enterprise environments and real delivery cycles.

items.name: GitHub Actions / Azure DevOps / Docker
items.detail: Automation of builds, deployments, and version control in production, with focus on repeatability and operational continuity.

items.name: OpenTelemetry / Application Insights
items.detail: Tracing and monitoring for technical diagnosis in distributed systems and environments with multiple integrations.

items.name: React / Next.js
items.detail: Real frontend and full stack experience for building complete solutions when the context requires going all the way to the interface layer.

items.name: Python / Django / Flask
items.detail: Used in tooling, APIs, and scenarios where the problem or team requires going beyond the main stack without losing technical consistency.

items.title: "Microservices do not fix a poorly designed system."
items.tag: They only distribute the cost of bad decisions.
items.body: If a team still does not control boundaries, data, and dependencies inside a monolith, splitting it into services adds coordination, network overhead, and more failure points.

items.title: "Architecture is not drawing boxes."
items.tag: It is deciding which complexity is worth paying for.
items.body: A lot of diagrams look fine until production happens. Useful architecture shows up when changes are still possible months later.

items.title: "Clean Architecture alone will not save you."
items.tag: Neat folders do not replace real boundaries.
items.body: You can have very clean layers and still be trapped in a fragile system if everything ends up knowing too much about everything else.

items.title: "Most systems do not die in a crash."
items.tag: They die when every change becomes slow and expensive.
items.body: Software can stay online for months while losing maintainability, clarity, and speed of evolution. That deterioration is also failure.

items.title: "The stack matters less than the ability to use it with judgment."
items.tag: Technology without judgment only changes the type of problem.
items.body: I am not interested in defending tools like a religion. I care about choosing them according to constraints, costs, and system shape.

items.title: "Knowing a language is not the same as solving problems."
items.body: You can master a framework and still not understand how to build a system. Tools change; modeling, boundaries, and evolution problems do not. If your value depends on one specific technology, it is fragile. Judgment is what actually scales across contexts.

contact.title: Ready to scale without the drama?
contact.description: If you need systems that remain maintainable as they grow, we should talk.
